projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

insights.summary: The consistent 18–19 device count paired with zero camera motion and late-night timing strongly indicates stationary smart home devices (routers, hubs, or tablets) are being miscounted as mobile phones, rather than actual occupancy or guests. This suggests a network tracking configuration issue rather than a security or presence event.

The sustained 18–19 device count with zero camera motion past midnight, combined with Roxanne’s confirmed sleep cycle, strongly suggests either a large group of guests who have settled down quietly or a persistent IoT device pairing anomaly; the home is currently inactive but occupied beyond the core family.

The sustained detection of 17–20 mobile devices late at night, significantly exceeding the household size of six, paired with zero camera motion, strongly indicates either an unannounced gathering with guests staying in rooms or a persistent presence detection anomaly. Verifying this will confirm whether the home is secure or if sensor calibration is needed.

The sustained high device count (17-20) combined with zero camera motion late Sunday night suggests either a network anomaly (unexpected connected devices) or the family gathered in a single room with low activity. This warrants a quick network security check or a gentle voice prompt to confirm everyone is accounted for.

The sustained high device count (17-19) paired with zero camera motion over the last two hours strongly suggests a network presence-tracking glitch or that the household is gathered in a single, camera-blind zone. Verify your network's active device list and camera feeds to rule out phantom connections or blind spots.

The late-afternoon device surge followed by sustained high presence and zero camera motion strongly indicates a planned evening gathering where guests are settled in common areas, likely explaining the lack of motion triggers; this aligns with typical Sunday weekend activity and requires no action.

Sustained high device presence (18-20) over the last hour contrasts with zero camera motion logs, suggesting cameras may be offline, motion detection is disabled, or presence data is skewed by a network anomaly. Verifying camera status is recommended to ensure monitoring integrity.

The connected device count surged from 12 to 20 between 16:24 and 16:29, indicating a rapid arrival of guests for a Sunday gathering. This sustained high presence count suggests the home is now hosting a social event, which may warrant proactive adjustments to climate, lighting, or security settings.

The persistent oscillation between 11–13 devices combined with zero camera activity during typical sleep hours (4–6 AM) strongly indicates presence-tracking drift (likely Bluetooth/WiFi polling artifacts) rather than actual household movement, suggesting the system may be overcounting and could trigger false automations if left unmonitored.

The persistent oscillation between 11 and 12 active mobile devices at 5 AM on a Sunday, combined with zero camera motion, strongly suggests a network glitch or a single device stuck in a reconnection loop rather than normal household activity. This warrants a quick network restart or checking for a rogue device to prevent battery drain or connectivity issues.
